Network Modeling Process
One-off for basic understanding
• Collect data
• Combine schemas and populate
• Make transitive associations within dataset
• De-duplicate data and schema
Iterative for real-time production
• Assets information export and direct scanning. Avoid middle-men.
• Scheduled auto updates
• Data and process flow
4
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Current Network Model Size
• Inner Network: ≈ 10,000 elements with 30 features = 300,000 data points
‣ device types and operating systems mapped to functionality: ≈ 150
‣ devices with mapped geolocations ≈ 5,000
‣ customer-related infrastructure ≈ 600 devices/ports identified
• Outer Network: ≈ 12,000 elements with 5 features = 60,000 data points
‣ identified network prefixes/netmasks company associations ≈ 9,600
‣ peer routing relationships ranked by volume ≈ 12,000
‣ route configuration statements catalogued ≈ 10,000
5
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
A Network Model Provides Understanding of What to Protect and How
• Understanding overall network structure
• Understanding node relationships
• Understanding of junctions, flows, vulnerabilities
• Plainly formatted data for use with simple tools for quick lookups
• Base data formatted to allow for import into a variety of advanced tools
6
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Graphics from Network Model Data
• Integrates with visualization tools to explore networks in new ways
• Selection of specific data sets as base data exports where graphing apps struggle
‣ Gephi
‣ Google Earth
‣ Matplotlib, Plotly, Bokeh, Seaborn, etc (Python)
‣ T-SNE and high dimensionality dataset clustering (future)

Example Core and Private Core Routers
8
Network model data fed through a local (off-line) instance of Google Earth to map
locations. Here we’ll show 2 types of nodes (core and private core routers) and fly
to locations in Singapore and New York. We’ll see data from the network model for
reference as we move to the locations.

Example Customer Infrastructure
9
Next we’ll look at the infrastructure supporting customers xxx and xxx. Note the
data model shows port level detail. Knowing the manufacturer, model, OS and port
of companies’ footprint on our network could help enable specific command
generation for protection against incidents and threats.

Example Customer Shared Infrastructure
10
We can see how xxx infrastructure is shared between multiple customers. This can be useful when
infrastructure impacting events occur: massive denial of service attacks, natural disasters, power outages, etc.

© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Allow External Events to be Correlated to Internal Assets and Services (hits).
• Threat intel and existing Security Log Management (SLM) infrastructure applied against a network data
model enables mapping of threats and events to physical assets and related customers.
‣ Traditional cyber-threat intel
‣ Environmental (floods, earthquake, power)
‣ Social/News
‣ HR incidents
‣ Other
12
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Hit Generation
• Match threat indicators to infrastructure
• Match company names to infrastructure
• Match IPs (to subnets via radix-sort) to customers and infrastructure
• Match employee threats to infrastructure
• Match customer threats to infrastructure
• Match news/media/social to infrastructure
… all aimed at developing an awareness of the threat environment and its specific, potential
impacts on xxx interests, as well as allowing for rapid response to those threats.
13
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Hit Results
• Alerts to config hands
• Specific command recommendations for internal assets to protect network and customers.
The ability to correlate threat and log information against inventoried customers and infrastructure enables
rapid response.
14
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Hit Predictions
• Output to predictor dataset for building up hit predictions
• Graphing of hit prediction probabilities enables more intuitive approach to threat forecasts.
.

Example Event Radar Map
16
Just as weather radar plots against specific dimensions of data (coordinates, pressure, moisture, etc). Multi-
dimensional data could be graphed into intuitive “map” forms using other data points (IP space, vendors,
industries, etc. Imagine the map above with the state borders replaced by IP space borders, or vendor model
space borders or industry vertical space borders, while the moving cloud/moisture graphic acts to represent
port scans, breach reports, command and control traffic, etc.
